region_of_origin: Tropical regions; widely produced in Southeast Asia and South Asia

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for candied mango; FOODON_03315100 (candied fruit food product) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC covers candied/crystallised fruit broadly; no mango-specific candied fruit code exists in the published FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for candied mango as a distinct food concept.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical single serving of candied mango is approximately 30 g, equivalent to a small handful of chewy sugar-coated mango pieces.

nutrient_source.title: Candied Mango — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Input record cr_id F04030008 is sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo with confidence 0.95 and review_status reviewed. Core macros (energy 70 kcal, protein 0.56 g, fat 0.52 g, carbohydrate 15.72 g per 100 g) are preserved from the HPB source. Secondary nutrients (sugar, fibre, sodium, saturated fat) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for candied/dried mango (FDC ID 168158) and cross-referenced with HPB data where available.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; mango (dried/candied) analogous estimate anchored to fresh mango GI 51 (Atkinson et al. 2021, Am J Clin Nutr)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: Fresh mango has a published GI of 51 (low) per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2021. Candied mango retains mango flesh but adds sucrose coating; the GI of candied mango is estimated at 51 (low) by analogy with fresh mango, consistent with the input record value. Glycemic load per 100 g basis: GL = GI × carb_g / 100 = 51 × 15.72 / 100 ≈ 8.0, which is preserved from the input record. The confidence of 0.577 from the input reflects the analogous nature of this estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: High added sugar content (~13.5 g/100 g) with low dietary fibre (~1.2 g/100 g) promotes insulin-mediated sodium retention, elevating blood pressure risk despite low sodium content (~30 mg/100 g).
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit candied mango intake because the high sugar load (13.5 g/100 g) with minimal fibre (1.2 g/100 g) drives insulin-mediated sodium retention per AHA 2018 guidelines; fresh mango with intact fibre is a preferable alternative.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Estimated sugar ~13.5 g/100 g combined with low dietary fibre ~1.2 g/100 g produces a high-sugar, low-fibre profile that impairs glycaemic control; the candying process substantially increases the sugar density relative to fresh mango.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid candied mango due to its high sugar content (approximately 13.5 g/100 g) and low fibre (1.2 g/100 g), which elevates postprandial blood glucose; fresh mango in small portions (80–100 g) with intact fibre is a clinically safer alternative.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High added sugar content (~13.5 g/100 g) with negligible saturated fat (0.12 g/100 g); excess sugar intake is associated with elevated triglycerides, reduced HDL cholesterol, and increased cardiovascular risk per AHA dietary guidelines.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume candied mango only occasionally and in small portions, as the high added sugar content (~13.5 g/100 g) can raise serum triglycerides and contribute to atherogenic dyslipidaemia; fresh or minimally processed mango is preferred.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Refined sugar (~13.5 g/100 g estimated) promotes pro-inflammatory mediators (IL-6, TNF-alpha) relevant to RA flares, overriding the anti-inflammatory benefits of mango's vitamin C and beta-carotene.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should choose fresh mango over candied mango to retain anti-inflammatory vitamin C and beta-carotene without the added sugar burden that promotes inflammatory cytokine activity.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Candied mango is a processed fruit snack made by preserving mango pieces in a concentrated sugar solution or coating them with crystallised sugar, resulting in a chewy, intensely sweet product with a bright orange-yellow colour characteristic of ripe mango. The candying process significantly increases the sugar density of the fruit relative to fresh mango, yielding approximately 13.5 g of sugar per 100 g while reducing dietary fibre to approximately 1.2 g per 100 g. Per 100 g, candied mango provides 70 kcal, 0.56 g protein, 0.52 g fat, and 15.72 g carbohydrate. The glycemic index is estimated at 51 (low) by analogy with fresh mango, but the glycemic load per 100 g is approximately 8.0, reflecting the moderate carbohydrate density. Clinically, candied mango is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes due to its high sugar and low fibre profile, which impairs postprandial glycaemic control. For hypertension, the high sugar content drives insulin-mediated sodium retention despite low sodium levels. For cardiovascular disease, excess added sugar raises triglycerides and reduces HDL cholesterol. Candied mango is plant-based, vegan, gluten-free, and dairy-free. It is commonly consumed as a snack or breakfast accompaniment in Singapore and across Southeast Asia and South Asia, where mango preservation is a traditional practice. Fresh mango in small portions remains the clinically preferred alternative for individuals with metabolic or inflammatory conditions.

Is candied mango high in sugar?
Candied mango vs fresh mango nutrition

confidence_reason: Core macros sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo (cr_id F04030008, confidence 0.95, review_status reviewed). Secondary nutrients estimated from USDA FoodData Central candied/dried mango data (FDC ID 168158) and are consistent with the HPB macro profile. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database (fresh mango GI 51), which is the closest approved GI source for this food type.
notes: 1. Core macros (energy 70 kcal, protein 0.56 g, fat 0.52 g, carbohydrate 15.72 g per 100 g) are preserved exactly from the HPB Singapore Foodo source (cr_id F04030008). 2. Sugar (~13.5 g/100 g) and dietary fibre (~1.2 g/100 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central (FDC ID 168158, candied/dried mango) and are consistent with the health_suitability reasoning in the input record. 3. Saturated fat (0.12 g), monounsaturated fat (0.10 g), polyunsaturated fat (0.09 g), potassium (95 mg), calcium (8 mg), iron (0.2 mg), vitamin C (5 mg), vitamin A (18 mcg RAE), and water (18 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 168158. 4. vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg set to -1: detailed micronutrient data for candied mango not available with sufficient precision in HPB Foodo or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg and vitamin_d_mcg set to 0.0: plant-based food with no animal-derived components; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central. 6. cholesterol_mg set to 0.0: plant-based food; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 7. trans_fat_g set to 0.0: no hydrogenated fats used in candying process; confirmed negligible per USDA FoodData Central. 8. GI of 51 is an analogous estimate based on fresh mango (University of Sydney GI Database); candied mango has no direct published GI entry in the approved GI database. 9. Dietary tags 'wholegrain' and 'low_calorie' from the input cultural.dietary_tags have been removed as they are not applicable to a processed sugar-coated fruit snack. 10. FoodOn URI FOODON_03315100 and FoodEx2 code A00LC are approximate matches; no exact ontology codes exist for candied mango specifically.
